value.Title: Morgan residents scrutinize solar farm proposed near Chapin
value.Overview: Jacksonville Journal-Courier (Illinois), STATE AND REGIONAL NEWS, 534words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJM-TDM3-S2P3-402F-00000-00Morgan residents scrutinize solar farm proposed near Chapin2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:57:11ZLexisNexisSTATE AND REGIONAL NEWSMorgan residents scrutinize solar farm proposed near ChapinBen SingsonJacksonville Journal-Courier, Ill.

May 11—A solar farm development planned for west of Chapin was the subject Monday of much discussion and criticism during a Morgan County Board of Commissioners meeting.

The board unanimously agreed during its meeting to a memorandum of understanding between itself and SG Chapin PV, a subsidiary of St. Louis-based solar company Solar Generation MW. The company wants to build a solar panel farm on 24 acres of land west of Chapin.

Solar Generation MW President Peter Yannakakis spoke about the project during the meeting. The solar panel farm would generate 4.59 megawatts of energy at peak output for a "community solar project" that would provide renewable energy to customers unable to put solar panels on their property, he said. The electricity would be sold to Ameren Illinois; customers who subscribe to the project would receive a 10% discount on their electricity bills, he said.

"It's kind of a win-win for all," Yannakakis said.

The farm would be built along Neelyville Road and Grants Lane, Yannakakis said. No vegetative screening would be put up to conceal the solar panels from view because there are no neighbors near its proposed construction site, he said. Construction would take from six to 12 months to complete and the company has taken out a 25-year lease on the land, he said.

Yannakakis fielded numerous questions during the meeting from both the board and county residents, some of whom were skeptical about what the company was putting forth. The farm is not part of Ameren's efforts to create large-load projects around the country, nor is the company working with any closed-loop data center programs, he said.

Solar Generation MW will issue a $300,000 bond to deconstruct the farm at the end of its lease and return the land to "the state it's in right now," Yannakakis said.

Some residents said that amount of money will not be enough to undo whatever damage construction of the farm could cause.

"How's $300,000 going to ... replace the nutrients that are lost?" county resident Kristy Clegg asked. "You're going to kill that ground. That ground is not going to be viable ground ever again."

Commissioner Michael Woods noted that the county can review the amount of money in the decommissioning bond 10 years after the solar panel farm goes online and every five years afterward.

Some residents also took issue with the lack of a plan for vegetative screening around the site.

Resident Cody Smith said a screen would provide habitats for local fauna, such as deer and pollinators. Lucy Reid of Jacksonville asked whether the company would plant screening if someone chose to move near the proposed site within the next 25 years, noting that residents often drive past the area.

"You're not doing it today, but we have to live here for 25 years," Reid said.

Board Chair Michael Wankel said the company is not required by the state to put up screening around the site; a county ordinance that would mandate it still is under review.

However, Yannakakis said he would talk with the county about adding screening to the site "with the plan of doing it."

value.Title: 24-acre solar farm proposed near Chapin

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJN-FYD3-SD1C-J1M8-00000-0024-acre solar farm proposed near Chapin2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:57:11ZLexisNexisAPg. A00124-acre solar farm proposed near ChapinBen SingsonStaff Writer

1) PHOTO: Peter Yannakakis (standing), president of Solar Generation MW, talks about a proposed solar panel farm that would be built west of Chapin. Morgan County Board of Commissioners agreed to a memorandum of understanding with a subsidiary of the company during its meeting Monday. (Ben Singson/Journal-Courier)

A solar farm development planned for west ofChapin was the subject Monday of much discussion and criticism during a Morgan County Board of Commissioners meeting.

The board unanimously agreed during its meeting to a memorandum of understanding between itself andSG Chapin PV, a subsidiary of St. Louis-based solar company Solar Generation MW. The company wants to build a solar panel farm on 24 acres of land west ofChapin.

Solar Generation MW President Peter Yannakakis spoke about the project during the meeting. The solar panel farm would generate 4.59 megawatts of energy at peak output for a "community solar project" that would provide renewable energy to customers unable to put solar panels on their property, he said. The electricity would be sold to Ameren Illinois; customers who subscribe to the project would receive a 10% discount on their electricity bills, he said.

"It's kind of a win-win for all," Yannakakis said.

The farm would be built alongNeelyville Road and Grants Lane, Yannakakis said. No vegetative screening would be put up to conceal the solar panels from view because there are no neighbors near its proposed construction site, he said. Construction would take from six to 12 months to complete and the company has taken out a 25-year lease on the land, he said.

Yannakakis fielded numerous questions during the meeting from both the board and county residents, some of whom were skeptical about what the company was putting forth. The farm is not part of Ameren's efforts to create large-load projects around the country, nor is the company working with any closed-loop data center programs, he said.

Solar Generation MW will issue a $300,000 bond to deconstruct the farm at the end of its lease and return the land to "the state it's in right now," Yannakakis said.

Some residents said that amount of money will not be enough to undo whatever damage construction of the farm could cause.

"How's $300,000 going to ... replace the nutrients that are lost?" county resident Kristy Clegg asked. "You're going to kill that ground. That ground is not going to be viable ground ever again."

Commissioner Michael Woods noted that the county can review the amount of money in the decommissioning bond 10 years after the solar panel farm goes online and every five years afterward.

Some residents also took issue with the lack of a plan for vegetative screening around the site.

Resident Cody Smith said a screen would provide habitats for local fauna, such as deer and pollinators. Lucy Reid of Jacksonville asked whether the company would plant screening if someone chose to move near the proposed site within the next 25 years, noting that residents often drive past the area.

"You're not doing it today, but we have to live here for 25 years," Reid said.

Board Chair Michael Wankel said the company is not required by the state to put up screening around the site; a county ordinance that would mandate it still is under review.

However, Yannakakis said he would talk with the county about adding screening to the site "with the plan of doing it."

value.Title: Plans for solar farms raise concerns in central Missouri

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHC-B093-S517-10KV-00000-00Plans for solar farms raise concerns in central Missouri2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:57:11ZLexisNexisNEWSPlans for solar farms raise concerns in central MissouriChris Howell chris.howell@sedaliademocrat.com

As solar energy development expands across rural Missouri, concerns are growing among residents around the region about the pace of projects and the lack of statewide regulation.

Megan McKenzie-Santiago, who lives near Pilot Grove and grew up in Cooper County, says her concerns aren't about opposing renewable energy outright but about what she sees as a regulatory gap.

"People have a right to do what they want with their property," McKenzie-Santiago said. "Property rights are a big thing to farmers and landowners. But what I've found in my research is that Missouri has no legislation on the books for green energy installation and how it should be handled."

That absence of statewide rules, she says, leaves both landowners and neighboring properties in uncertain territory.

"Our concern is that there are no regulations these companies have to follow as they come into Missouri," McKenzie-Santiago said. "And in rural counties, there's generally no zoning either."

While many rural residents oppose heavy zoning restrictions, McKenzie-Santiago said there is still a need for environmental safeguards and consideration of neighboring properties.

"You have to ask what rights do neighboring landowners have if these developments start affecting their land?" McKenzie-Santiago said. "There are concerns about erosion, groundwater, and even potential contamination if panels are damaged by storms."

Those concerns have been amplified by recent severe weather across central Missouri.

"There's always the possibility of tornadoes or hail," McKenzie-Santiago said. "What happens if these panels are damaged? What's released into the environment?"

McKenzie-Santiago said interest in the issue intensified after reports of surveyors and drones operating near Pilot Grove private property earlier this spring.

"That was witnessed by multiple landowners," McKenzie-Santiago said. "There were rumors before, but people didn't want to go off rumors. Then they started seeing activity."

That activity led to a series of local meetings, including a community gathering attended by county commissioners and state officials. Among them was State Rep. Tim Taylor, as well as discussions referencing legislative efforts by Rep. Brad Pollitt.

At the state level, attention has also turned to a proposal from Sen. Cindy O'Laughlin, who has advanced SB 849, a bill that could temporarily halt new solar developments.

"The idea of a moratorium is a step in the right direction," McKenzie-Santiago said. "It would give the state time to actually put regulations in place and understand the long-term impacts."

She emphasized that the issue goes beyond solar panels alone, pointing to broader concerns about energy infrastructure and emerging technologies.

"If you look at what's happening in other states, there are connections between large-scale energy production and things like AI data centers," McKenzie-Santiago said. "Those facilities require huge amounts of power and water, and that raises additional concerns for rural communities."

Missouri's agricultural identity also plays a role in the debate.

"This is a major ag region," McKenzie-Santiago said. "We're home to the Missouri State Fair. Agriculture is a huge part of our economy, and people want to make sure that's protected."

For residents trying to make sense of the issue, McKenzie-Santiago encourages independent research and civic engagement.

"When I started looking into this, most of the information online was from solar companies themselves," McKenzie-Santiago said. "People need to ask questions, where is the funding coming from, who is manufacturing these panels, and what happens at the end of their lifespan?"

She also pointed to policies in other states, such as Washington, which requires companies to have end-of-life plans for solar panels, including buyback or recycling programs.

"That's something Missouri doesn't have right now," McKenzie-Santiagoe said. "And without it, there are still a lot of unanswered questions."

At the local level, she noted that county commissions may have limited, but important authority.

"Even without state legislation, there are some statutes that allow county commissioners to slow things down," McKenzie-Santiago said. "They do have some power, and that's something people should be aware of."

As discussions continue, McKenzie-Santiago said her goal is not to stop development entirely, but to ensure it is done thoughtfully.

"This isn't about killing green energy," McKenzie-Santiago said. "It's about making sure we understand it, regulate it properly, and protect the people and land that are already here."

Rep. Brad Pollitt said he had heard of the solar farm planned 30 miles from Sedalia and is rushing to push through a bill to offer some statewide regulation of the solar industry in Missouri.

"Right now we don't have any regulations and that's what we're working on with this bill,trying to just put some regulations out there for the state," Pollitt said. "Not trying to stop them, you know, it's a property rights issue but just something for the people who are around it to know what the state regulations are."

Without statewide regulations, solar farms could be headed to the area leaving little recourse for neighboring farmers when disputes arise.

If the bill does pass, county commissioners could end up being the last line of defense.

"It says in the bill that if a county wanted to do a Chapter 100, then they could change those regulations," Pollitt said. "But we would just give them a skeleton framework on if you want to just go with the state regulations here it is; it talks about setbacks and taxation and those types of things but you know we're still working on it, two weeks left in the session and there's obviously some opposition to the bill or would have been moving a little better."

For more information visit the Pilot Grove - Solar Field Project Conversations and Concerns Facebook page, bit.ly/4d2zssB.

Chris Howell can be reached at 660-530-0146

value.Title: Plans for solar farms raise concerns, calls for action

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JH5-BR33-RV78-62J1-00000-00Plans for solar farms raise concerns, calls for action2026-05-04T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:52:32ZLexisNexisCITY & COUNTYPlans for solar farms raise concerns, calls for actionChris Howell chris.howell@sedaliademocrat.com

As solar energy development expands across rural Missouri, concerns are growing among residents in and around Pettis County about the pace of projects and the lack of statewide regulation.

Megan McKenzie-Santiago, who lives near Pilot Grove and grew up in Cooper County, says her concerns aren't about opposing renewable energy outright but about what she sees as a regulatory gap.

"People have a right to do what they want with their property," McKenzie-Santiago said. "Property rights are a big thing to farmers and landowners. But what I've found in my research is that Missouri has no legislation on the books for green energy installation and how it should be handled."

That absence of statewide rules, she says, leaves both landowners and neighboring properties in uncertain territory.

"Our concern is that there are no regulations these companies have to follow as they come into Missouri," McKenzie-Santiago said. "And in rural counties, there's generally no zoning either."

While many rural residents oppose heavy zoning restrictions, McKenzie-Santiago said there is still a need for environmental safeguards and consideration of neighboring properties.

"You have to ask what rights do neighboring landowners have if these developments start affecting their land?" McKenzie-Santiago said. "There are concerns about erosion, groundwater, and even potential contamination if panels are damaged by storms."

Those concerns have been amplified by recent severe weather across central Missouri.

"There's always the possibility of tornadoes or hail," McKenzie-Santiago said. "What happens if these panels are damaged? What's released into the environment?"

McKenzie-Santiago said interest in the issue intensified after reports of surveyors and drones operating near Pilot Grove private property earlier this spring.

"That was witnessed by multiple landowners," McKenzie-Santiago said. "There were rumors before, but people didn't want to go off rumors. Then they started seeing activity."

That activity led to a series of local meetings, including a community gathering attended by county commissioners and state officials. Among them was State Rep. Tim Taylor, as well as discussions referencing legislative efforts by Rep. Brad Pollitt.

At the state level, attention has also turned to a proposal from Sen. Cindy O'Laughlin, who has advanced SB 849, a bill that could temporarily halt new solar developments.

"The idea of a moratorium is a step in the right direction," McKenzie-Santiago said. "It would give the state time to actually put regulations in place and understand the long-term impacts."

She emphasized that the issue goes beyond solar panels alone, pointing to broader concerns about energy infrastructure and emerging technologies.

"If you look at what's happening in other states, there are connections between large-scale energy production and things like AI data centers," McKenzie-Santiago said. "Those facilities require huge amounts of power and water, and that raises additional concerns for rural communities."

Missouri's agricultural identity also plays a role in the debate, particularly in counties like Pettis and Cooper.

"This is a major ag region," McKenzie-Santiago said. "We're home to the Missouri State Fair. Agriculture is a huge part of our economy, and people want to make sure that's protected."

For residents trying to make sense of the issue, McKenzie-Santiago encourages independent research and civic engagement.

"When I started looking into this, most of the information online was from solar companies themselves," McKenzie-Santiago said. "People need to ask questions, where is the funding coming from, who is manufacturing these panels, and what happens at the end of their lifespan?"

She also pointed to policies in other states, such as Washington, which requires companies to have end-of-life plans for solar panels, including buyback or recycling programs.

"That's something Missouri doesn't have right now," McKenzie-Santiagoe said. "And without it, there are still a lot of unanswered questions."

At the local level, she noted that county commissions may have limited, but important authority.

"Even without state legislation, there are some statutes that allow county commissioners to slow things down," McKenzie-Santiago said. "They do have some power, and that's something people should be aware of."

As discussions continue, McKenzie-Santiago said her goal is not to stop development entirely, but to ensure it is done thoughtfully.

"This isn't about killing green energy," McKenzie-Santiago said. "It's about making sure we understand it, regulate it properly, and protect the people and land that are already here."

Rep. Brad Pollitt said he had heard of the solar farm planned 30 miles from Sedalia and is rushing to push through a bill to offer some statewide regulation of the solar industry in Missouri.

"Right now we don't have any regulations and that's what we're working on with this bill,trying to just put some regulations out there for the state," Pollitt said. "Not trying to stop them, you know, it's a property rights issue but just something for the people who are around it to know what the state regulations are."

Without statewide regulations, solar farms could be headed to the area leaving little recourse for neighboring farmers when disputes arise.

If the bill does pass, county commissioners could end up being the last line of defense.

"It says in the bill that if a county wanted to do a Chapter 100, then they could change those regulations," Pollitt said. "But we would just give them a skeleton framework on if you want to just go with the state regulations here it is; it talks about setbacks and taxation and those types of things but you know we're still working on it, two weeks left in the session and there's obviously some opposition to the bill or would have been moving a little better."

For more information visit the Pilot Grove - Solar Field Project Conversations and Concerns Facebook page, bit.ly/4d2zssB.

Chris Howell can be reached at 660-530-0146

Potential solar farms across the region are a topic of discussion, including in the Pilot Grove community in Cooper County. In this file photo, Farmland is seen with solar panels from Cypress Creek Renewables in Thurmont, Md. Photo by Joshua A. Bickel | AP Photo

value.Title: Metro seeks public input on bus rapid transit routes

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHR-F973-SCCF-M07R-00000-00Metro seeks public input on bus rapid transit routes2026-05-28T15:57:11Z2026-05-28T15:57:11ZLexisNexisBUSINESSMetro seeks public input on bus rapid transit routesSt. Louis American staff

Business briefs

Metro seeks public input on bus rapid transit routes

Metro Transit is advancing two final route options for a proposed bus rapid transit line connecting north and south St. Louis, with planners seeking public feedback this week through open houses and a virtual meeting.

According to the St. Louis Business Journal, the proposed line would run along Jefferson Avenue and North Florissant Road and is designed to provide faster, more reliable service through dedicated bus lanes and limited stops.

The St. Louis Post-Dispatch reported the two remaining route options would extend farther east downtown than the previously abandoned MetroLink Green Line proposal.

Metro officials will host open house sessions from 5 to 7 p.m. Tuesday at Rung for Women and from 5 to 7 p.m. Wednesday at the Polish Heritage Center. A virtual meeting is scheduled for 7 p.m. Thursday.

Staff members will answer questions and gather public input, but no formal presentation is planned.

St. Louis proposes stricter rules for data centers

St. Louis city officials have proposed tougher zoning rules for future data centers, including a requirement that every project receive case-by-case approval instead of being automatically allowed in certain industrial areas.

Under the proposal released Tuesday, all data centers would be treated as "conditional uses," meaning developers would need city approval before construction could move forward. Supporters say the process gives officials and residents a chance to review issues such as noise, energy use, water consumption, traffic and neighborhood impact before projects are approved.

The updated framework would also require community benefits agreements for large facilities, along with environmental impact studies and job and tax revenue estimates. According to the St. Louis Business Journal, the city also tightened renewable energy standards and lowered the threshold for projects classified as "major" data centers. The proposal heads to the city Planning Commission later this month.

St. Louis advances $1,000 bonuses for city employees

St. Louis officials have taken an initial step toward approving one-time $1,000 bonuses for most city employees as leaders continue grappling with staffing shortages and retention challenges across municipal departments.

The Board of Aldermen's Budget and Public Employees Committee advanced the proposal last week. Supporters said the payments would recognize workers who continued providing city services despite vacancies and increasing workloads. Elected officials would not qualify for the bonuses.

The proposal still requires approval from the full Board of Aldermen before going to Mayor Cara Spencer for consideration.

City officials have said the payments would be funded through existing budget resources rather than new taxes or borrowing.

United Way, Battlehawks renew community partnership

The United Way of GreaterSt. Louis and the St. Louis Battlehawks have renewed their partnership for a third year, continuing efforts tied to volunteer work, fundraising and community outreach.

Under the agreement, the Battlehawks will use United Way's Volunteer Center to coordinate player and team service activities. The partnership also includes a "Hero of the Game" recognition at home games honoring community volunteers.

United Way will also receive a portion of proceeds from select regular-season and postseason ticket sales through a fundraising arrangement tied to Battlehawks games.

Battlehawks defensive end Travis Feeney is also partnering with the organization on community initiatives.

Legal Services gala marks 70 years of advocacy

Legal Services of Eastern Missouri will celebrate its 70th anniversary with a gala Saturday, May 9, at the The Ritz-Carlton, St. Louis in Clayton.

The event will highlight the nonprofit's legal advocacy work across eastern Missouri, including housing, domestic violence and economic development initiatives.

Organizers said the program will also recognize long-running efforts such as the Community Economic Development Microenterprise Program and the Neighborhood Advocacy Program.

Legal Services provides civil legal assistance across 21 counties in eastern Missouri. The annual gala is expected to draw attorneys, business leaders and civic organizations.

The post Metro seeks public input on bus rapid transit routes appeared first on St. Louis American.

value.Title: Google Signs 500 MW Solar Deal to Power Texas Data Centers

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJW-F1P3-RTDX-N21M-00000-00Google Signs 500 MW Solar Deal to Power Texas Data Centers2026-05-13T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:57:11ZLexisNexisGoogle Signs 500 MW Solar Deal to Power Texas Data Centers

Google announced that it has signed a 15-year Power Purchase Agreement (PPA) with renewable energy developer and independent power producer Linea Energy for the production of 500 MW of energy from a new solar project in Texas, aimed at supporting the companys data center operations in the state.

The announcement marks the latest in a series of large-scale clean energy purchases by Google, including a 1 GW solar PPA signed in February with TotalEnergies in Texas, and a 1.2 GW deal with Clearway in January, to provide power to data centers in Missouri, Texas, and West Virginia.

Googles environmental targets include 2030 goals to reach net zero emissions across its operations and value chain, and its 24/7 CFE ambition, aiming to run its entire business on carbon-free energy (CFE) by 2030, matching electricity demand with CFE supply every hour of every day, in every region where the company operates. The company has signed more than 170 agreements to purchase over 23 GW of clean energy generation since 2010.

Will Conkling, Director of Energy and Power, Google said:

By collaborating with Linea Energy to bring new low-cost power to the grid, we are helping to ensure the Lone Star States energy system remains affordable for local families and businesses.

The new agreement will source power from Lineas Duffy Solar Project, which will cover 3,526 acres in Matagorda, Texas, supporting Googles data center operations in the Electric Reliability Council of Texas (ERCOT) market, a wholesale electric market which covers 90% of the states electrical grid and serves around 26 million customers.

The project will begin construction in Q3 2026 and is co-located with the 235 MWac Duffy battery energy storage system (BESS) project currently under construction.

Cassidy DeLine, Chief Executive Officer of Linea Energy said:

We are pleased to sign this agreement to supply clean energy in Texas to Google, one of the worlds largest buyers of clean power. This transaction highlights Lineas credibility amongst the largest hyperscalers and the ability to support affordability and grid reliability.

value.Title: US company takes over Megger Group

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK4-CW53-RTFJ-G03T-00000-00US company takes over Megger Group2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:43:14ZLexisNexisUS company takes over Megger GroupSam Lennon

By Sam Lennon

slennon@thekmgroup.co.uk

A US company which is taking over an electrical testing equipment manufacturer says its Dover base it "critical" to its operation.

Engineering firm ESCO Technologies Ltd, based in Missouri, is in the process of acquiring the Megger Group, where 260 work at its Dover headquarters.

The deal is estimated at $2.35 billion (£1.72 billion) and is expected to be completed by December.

A spokesperson for ESCO said: "The Dover site is critical to maintaining Megger's strong presence in the United Kingdom and international markets, which are central to ESCO's long term growth plans.

"We are now in the regulatory approval process.

"We anticipate finalising the acquisition in the first quarter of our financial year 2027, October to December 2026."

ESCO Technologies provides engineered products in areas such as defence, aerospace, consumer electronics, healthcare and renewable energy industries.

ESCO says Megger will become part of its Utility Solution Group (USG) segment. USG products and services include battery, cable, circuit breaker, relay, transformer, and motor test equipment, online monitoring solutions, and data analytics for grid and electric power assets.

Bryan Sayler, president and chief executive of ESCO Technologies, added: "We have long admired Megger and view it as an exceptional strategic fit within our USG portfolio.

"Megger adds a respected and differentiated product portfolio, with highly complementary capabilities, deep technical expertise, and strong customer and supplier relationships."

The deal involves a merger between Megger and ESCO's Doble Engineering brand.

A statement from Megger said: "Together, these brands bring an unrivalled reputation of innovation, expertise, and trust by their customers.

"This is to deliver a unified platform for holistic electrical asset management on a global scale."

Megger, whose corporate headquarters is in Archcliffe Road, Dover, designs, develops, tests and manufactures electrical equipment.

It has been based in Dover since 1966. The company also has branches in countries such as Sweden, Germany and in the United States in Texas, Colorado and Pennsylvania.

It globally hires about 1,500 people.

value.Title: Report: CT utility company wrongly rejects solar procurement

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJW-4953-SGN1-G1MS-00000-00Report: CT utility company wrongly rejects solar procurement2026-05-13T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:44:42ZLexisNexisNEWSReport: CT utility company wrongly rejects solar procurementEdwin J. Viera

An Acadia Center report finds Eversource walking away from a multi-state solar procurement deal could block over 100 new, clean MW from coming online, and jeopardize other states from joining similar procurements in the future. (Adobe Stock)

Listen to this story

0:0002:00

Connecticut ratepayers could lose out on major savings because of what some affordability advocates call a miscalculation by power provider Eversource.

An Acadia Center report finds the company is abandoning a multi-state, 54-megawatt solar procurement plan, citing high development costs. It also finds those contracts would potentially save ratepayers $80 million over the next 20 years.

Kate McAuliffe, senior policy advocate for Connecticut at the Acadia Center, said Eversource has a double standard for solar projects, but not for its asset condition projects.

"Those can amount to as much as $300 million in spending annually," she said. "When you compare that to the annual costs of these solar projects, it's pretty small, and yet we're not hearing the same level of objections when they look to recover the costs of those asset condition projects."

These projects are mostly rebuilds of existing assets, making them a factor in Connecticut's high utility bills. This comes as the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission issued an order calling for several utilities, including Eversource and Avangrid, to refund $1.5 billion to consumers. However, the two utility companies asked FERC and the U.S. Court of Appeals to stay the decision.

Renewable-energy buildouts often face challenges. While the projects come with large up-front costs, the long-term benefits and cost-saving measures pay off in the end. Yet, the Acadia Center report notes renewables are a reliable boogeyman for utility companies regarding affordability.

Noah Berman, senior policy advocate and utility innovation program manager at the Acadia Center, said Eversource scapegoats these developments because it can't make money on them.

"Those other pieces of the bill frequently are actually saving money for ratepayers," he said, "because, unfortunately, the way electricity bills work is you see the gross cost, but you don't see the net benefit on the bill. However, those net benefits still exist."

Data show states with more renewable energy have seen smaller electricity bill increases over the last two decades, while states more reliant on natural gas have higher prices. This comes as Connecticut has seen declining prices in the last year, according to the Energy Information Administration. But its prices are still some of the nation's highest.

value.Title: Google Signs 500 MW Solar Deal to Power Texas Data Centers
value.Overview: ESG Today, TECH LATEST & GOOGLE NEWS, 353words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJN-P3Y3-S4C7-34XG-00000-00Google Signs 500 MW Solar Deal to Power Texas Data Centers2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:57:11ZLexisNexisTECH LATEST & GOOGLE NEWSGoogle Signs 500 MW Solar Deal to Power Texas Data CentersKenny Fisher

Link to Image

Google announced that it has signed a 15-year Power Purchase Agreement (PPA) with renewable energy developer and independent power producer Linea Energy for the production of 500 MW of energy from a new solar project in Texas, aimed at supporting the company's data center operations in the state.

The announcement marks the latest in a series of large-scale clean energy purchases by Google, including a 1 GW solar PPA signed in February with TotalEnergies in Texas, and a 1.2 GW deal with Clearway in January, to provide power to data centers in Missouri, Texas, and West Virginia.

Google's environmental targets include 2030 goals to reach net zero emissions across its operations and value chain, and its 24/7 CFE ambition, aiming to run its entire business on carbon-free energy (CFE) by 2030, matching electricity demand with CFE supply every hour of every day, in every region where the company operates. The company has signed more than 170 agreements to purchase over 23 GW of clean energy generation since 2010.

Will Conkling, Director of Energy and Power, Google said:

"By collaborating with Linea Energy to bring new low-cost power to the grid, we are helping to ensure the Lone Star State's energy system remains affordable for local families and businesses."

The new agreement will source power from Linea's Duffy Solar Project, which will cover 3,526 acres in Matagorda, Texas, supporting Google's data center operations in the Electric Reliability Council of Texas (ERCOT) market, a wholesale electric market which covers 90% of the state's electrical grid and serves around 26 million customers.

The project will begin construction in Q3 2026 and is co-located with the 235 MWac Duffy battery energy storage system (BESS) project currently under construction.

Cassidy DeLine, Chief Executive Officer of Linea Energy said:

"We are pleased to sign this agreement to supply clean energy in Texas to Google, one of the world's largest buyers of clean power. This transaction highlights Linea's credibility amongst the largest hyperscalers and the ability to support affordability and grid reliability."

urn:contentItem:6JK4-M4G3-RRYS-M1GN-00000-00Data center regulations weighed; some worry over jobs, energy, taxes2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:49:14ZLexisNexisRENEWABLE ENERGY NEWSData center regulations weighed; some worry over jobs, energy, taxesBy Sean Reed | The Center Square

(The Center Square) - Major bills in both the state Senate and House may heavily regulate data centers in the state. With the proposal spanning hundreds of pages, both those in favor and against the bill have a lot of arguments.

With a little more than two weeks of legislative session remaining, lawmakers have continued to weigh if and how they might regulate data centers over energy consumption and related issues.

A House Committee heard from multiple environmental organizations in favor of the 632-page bill on data centers late Tuesday.

House Bill 5513 would expand what permits data centers will be required to obtain, require them to report their energy use, pay into community funds and plan to save water and while bringing new energy capacity to the grid, according to Rep. Carol Ammons, D-Urbana.

Andrew Rehn and Tyshianna Bankhead, both representing member organizations of the Illinois Clean Jobs Coalition, and Brian McDaniel from Citizens Utility Board all spoke in favor of the plan.

Rehn said water consumption by data centers could leave Illinoisans without vital resources.

"Illinois lacks meaningful protections against large water users like data centers overusing our water and unlike many of our neighboring states, we do not regulate or meaningfully monitor water usage or overconsumption," Rehn said.

McDaniel favors the act because it will force new data center projects to bring renewable energy projects alongside development, expanding grid capacity as usage increases.

"Large data centers can increase these costs for all of us on different levels of the power system, from the utility level, the distribution level, to the high-voltage transmission lines, all the way to the markets that set our prices for power generation," McDaniel said.

Questions arose about data center power usage, along with associated rate hikes.

State Rep. Blaine Wilhour, R-Louisville, noted that recent rate hikes being associated with data centers did not happen in a vacuum.

"You've got to take that into the context of the capacity that had been previously taken off the market because of legislation that this side of the table supported," Wilhour said, referring to Democrats' support of the Climate and Equitable Jobs Act.

Brad Tietz from the Data Center Coalition and Joe Duffy from Climate Jobs Illinois were against the legislation, though for varying reasons.

Duffy, representing trade unions and other members of the renewable energy industry, said those he represents oppose the bill because the bill doesn't strongly enough regulate how the required energy projects will be built.

"We have concerns about several provisions," Duffy said. "A data center developer could meet Illinois' clean energy obligations by building a non-union solar project in Ohio, Missouri, or Indiana, for example, then count it toward their Illinois requirements, collect Illinois incentives, and generate none of the benefits here."

Tietz, however, opposes the bill on behalf of data center interests. He said new projects will be highly discouraged from choosing Illinois as the place to build, pushing jobs to neighboring states.

"Every data center is different. They have different purposes, different workloads, and activities occurring in them and different customers," Tietz said.

Tietz went on to say the bill is restrictive on all centers, even though they are not all created equal.

Tietz also said new data center projects have vastly contributed to the state's tax base and economy in recent years, providing jobs in various sectors statewide.

value.Title: Multiple 'hyperscale' data centers in the works around KC. What that means, see map
value.Overview: The Kansas City Star (Missouri), KANSAS CITY NEWS, 580words

urn:contentItem:6JJF-BB33-S4BM-D3KS-00000-00Multiple 'hyperscale' data centers in the works around KC. What that means, see map2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:45:59ZLexisNexisKANSAS CITY NEWSMultiple 'hyperscale' data centers in the works around KC. What that means, see mapSofi Zeman

As data center projects sprout up across the Kansas City metropolitan area, so are residents' concerns over what that means for their health and natural resources.

How much area water do they use? How will this new wave of development affect energy demand and prices? What are the risks of building something that relies on burning fossil fuels?

Those questions - which have been raised in local rooms from Independence to Smithville to Spring Hill - come at a time when technological advancements and Donald Trump's presidency have altered the way the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency regulates industry.

The number of data centers - facilities used to house computer systems and optimize internet efficiency - are surging in the United States as new tech gains momentum and companies become increasingly reliant on artificial intelligence.

Numerous data center projects that are eclipsing the size of older models are planned for the next few years in the KC metro, and some are already operating.

On both sides of the state line, in Kansas and Missouri , legislators have signaled that they want to encourage data center development through tax breaks designed for the facilities.

While national-level organizations are suing the EPA in an effort to get stronger federal safeguards, some local advocates around KC are calling on community and state officials to push developers to operate using clean energy sources alone.

Hyperscale data centers

Data center companies are in a global race to produce more AI, faster. And although some have pledged to lean into renewable energy, many rely on increasingly-strained power grids and fossil fuel-powered turbines to get ahead. Energy demand, thus the amount of combustion or other sources required to operate a data center, depends on scale.

More and more often, new data center projects are being described as hyperscale. That's a term that has slightly different definitions depending on what side of the state line someone's on. Kansas classifies projects as hyperscale if they use more than 75 megawatts of energy to operate. Legislation proposed in Missouri's defined hyperscale data centers as using more than 25 megawatts . Evergy defines "large load" projects as exceeding 75 megawatts.

Of the at least 10 large data center project proposals pending in the Kansas City metro, at least five are planned to operate using 100 or more megawatts, according to project proposals, company and city representatives and data compiled from Data Center Map, a site that has tracked such projects, pending and existing, since 2007.

Companies like Meta, which has a center operating in the Northland, have said they plan to match their data center use using entirely clean and renewable energy. Nebius, which is building a large, at least 800 MW facility out in Independence, will utilize some renewable energy while also leaning heavily on fossil fuel turbines.

Hyperscale data centers require notably higher amounts of power to operate than smaller facilities around the metro.

They also need more backup generators.

Data centers use emergency backup generators for reliability purposes, like during grid failures. Those backup generators, often powered using combustion turbines, are associated with pollution in the form of carbon monoxide, nitrogen oxides, volatile organic compounds, particulate matter and some hazardous air pollutants, according to the EPA.

See the map of which larger data centers are up and running, and which are coming.

urn:contentItem:6JJP-F6J3-RS96-T4SN-00000-00Evergy Inc. (NASDAQ: EVRG) Projects 7% to 8% Annual Retail Sales Growth Through 2030 Fueled by Data Center Expansion2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:57:11ZLexisNexisEvergy Inc. (NASDAQ: EVRG) Projects 7% to 8% Annual Retail Sales Growth Through 2030 Fueled by Data Center Expansion

AMERICAN DAILY STOCK REPORT

UNITED STATES EDITION

11 May 2026 14:16 EDT

Evergy Inc. (NASDAQ: EVRG) is projecting retail sales to grow 7% to 8% annually through 2030, raising its previous guidance of 6% as data center expansion drives demand. The outlook was shared during Evergy's May 8, 2026 quarterly earnings call, with executives highlighting the impact of large-load ESAs on revenue. The company disclosed a new large-load ESA with an unnamed customer, lifting total large-load ESAs to 2.5 GW from 1.9 GW in the prior quarter.

Evergy also said it is in talks to secure at least one more ESA within the year. It has already contracted an additional 450 MW with major customers, including Panasonic's EV battery plant, and discussions are underway to expand ESAs by up to 1.5 GW, with a plan to add 1.5 GW to 3 GW of new large-load customers after 2030. In the first quarter of 2026, Evergy reported income of $151.5 million, or 64 cents per share, up 21% from $125 million, or 54 cents per share, a year earlier, while revenue remained flat at $1.4 billion. The company attributed the earnings gain to a Kansas rate increase, higher non-regulated energy marketing revenue, and lower tax expenses, though depreciation and maintenance costs rose and pressured profitability. To meet growing demand, Evergy revised its capital expenditure plan to $21.6 billion, targeting 4.7 GW of gas-fired generation in Missouri and reducing long-term renewable energy plans by more than 90%, citing renewable project costs and regulatory changes as drivers for the shift.

Total dividends per share paid in the 12 months ended February 24 were $2.03.

SECTION 2 EVERGY ACTIVITIES

Activities

Evergy Inc., headquartered in Kansas, is a prominent electric utility company serving approximately 1.6 million customers across Kansas and Missouri. Its core business centers on the generation, transmission, distribution, and sale of electricity through a diversified energy portfolio that includes coal, natural gas, nuclear, and renewable sources. The company plays a vital role in regional energy reliability and sustainability, with a strong emphasis on modernizing its infrastructure to enhance efficiency and reduce emissions. Among its fastest-growing segments are renewable energy initiatives, particularly wind and solar projects, which have expanded rapidly due to increasing demand for clean power and supportive regulatory policies. This strategic focus positions Evergy to capitalize on the transition to a low-carbon economy, supporting long-term growth and innovation in the utility sector. It is the NASDAQ's largest Publishing company by market capitalisation.

Shares of REX American Resources Corporation ( NYSE:REX - Get Free Report) reached a new 52-week high during mid-day trading on Tuesday . The company traded as high as $51.36 and last traded at $51.2160, with a volume of 171574 shares traded. The stock had previously closed at $49.97.

Wall Street Analyst Weigh In

Separately, Weiss Ratings upgraded shares of REX American Resources from a "hold (c+)" rating to a "buy (b-)" rating in a research report on Monday, March 2nd. One investment analyst has rated the stock with a Buy rating, According to data from MarketBeat, REX American Resources presently has an average rating of "Buy".

The stock's 50-day moving average is $41.78 and its 200-day moving average is $36.19. The firm has a market capitalization of $1.69 billion, a price-to-earnings ratio of 20.49 and a beta of 0.63.

REX American Resources ( NYSE:REX - Get Free Report) last released its quarterly earnings data on Thursday, March 26th. The energy company reported $1.32 earnings per share (EPS) for the quarter, topping analysts' consensus estimates of $0.14 by $1.18. The business had revenue of $157.96 million during the quarter, compared to analysts' expectations of $162.00 million. REX American Resources had a net margin of 12.75% and a return on equity of 12.72%.

Insider Transactions at REX American Resources

In other REX American Resources news, CFO Douglas Bruggeman sold 2,000 shares of the company's stock in a transaction that occurred on Thursday, April 2nd. The shares were sold at an average price of $45.44, for a total value of $90,880.00. Following the completion of the transaction, the chief financial officer directly owned 259,170 shares in the company, valued at approximately $11,776,684.80. This represents a 0.77% decrease in their position. The sale was disclosed in a legal filing with the SEC, which can be accessed through this link. Company insiders own 13.91% of the company's stock.

REX American Resources Company Profile

( Get Free Report)

REX American Resources Corp. is a diversified agribusiness and renewable energy company headquartered in Kansas City, Missouri. Founded in 2005 through a reorganization of existing agricultural interests, the company focuses on two primary business segments: fuel ethanol production and specialty ingredient solutions. REX American Resources leverages its integrated operations to supply clean-burning fuel, animal feed co-products and sweetener ingredients to a broad customer base in North America and beyond.

In its alcohol fuels segment, the company operates an anhydrous ethanol production facility in Colwich, Kansas.

value.Title: CLAYCO CONSTRUCTION COMPANY, INC. - COMPREHENSIVE INDUSTRY AND PEER NEWS UPDATES

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK8-2JG3-SBS5-021G-00000-00CLAYCO CONSTRUCTION COMPANY, INC. - COMPREHENSIVE INDUSTRY AND PEER NEWS UPDATES2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:49:10ZLexisNexisSTOCKCLAYCO CONSTRUCTION COMPANY, INC. - COMPREHENSIVE INDUSTRY AND PEER NEWS UPDATES

DASHBOARD: CLAYCO CONSTRUCTION COMPANY

PermID4296495921Websitehttp://www.claycorp.comIndustryConstructionAddress2199 Innerbelt Business Center Drive Overland, Missouri 63114

ACTIVITIES:

Clayco Construction Company, Inc., founded in 1984 by Bob Clark, is a design-build firm headquartered in St. Louis, Missouri. With over 3,800 employees and $7.6 billion in 2024 revenue, it serves commercial, industrial, and life sciences sectors. Clayco offers architecture, engineering, and construction services through subsidiaries like Lamar Johnson Collaborative and Concrete Strategies. Known for projects like the Novus Headquarters, it emphasizes sustainable, LEED-certified buildings. In 2024, Clayco expanded into renewable energy and data center construction. Its culture fosters innovation, safety, and diversity, with robust training programs. Competing with DPR Construction, Clayco stands out for its integrated approach and fast-track delivery. Recent initiatives include advancing technology-driven construction and eco-friendly practices.

+ CLAYCO CONSTRUCTION COMPANY [3 News That Matters]

+ CHICAGO BUILDER CLAYCO BETS ON SOLAR AND STORAGE WITH NEW NATIONAL ARM

Clayco, a Chicago-based contractor, is expanding into solar and storage with a new national division, announced on 4th March 2026. The venture, known as Clayco Power and Energy, aims to create nearly 1,000 jobs nationwide by 2027 and generate about $300 million in annual revenue.The unit will oversee design, engineering, construction, and procurement for solar and storage projects and is already advancing a 350-acre Manteno, Illinois site, with construction slated to begin this June.The expansion comes as U.S. electricity demand is forecast to rise 15%–20% by 2030, with solar and storage contributing a growing share of new capacity after accounting for roughly 85% of last year’s additions.

+ IDAHO NATIONAL LAB TO HOST NUCLEAR-POWERED AI DATA CENTER: CLAYCO TO LEAD DOE PROPOSAL DESIGN-BUILD EFFORT

Clayco, a leading design-build firm specializing in complex mission-critical facilities, has announced its collaboration with Swiss-American energy company Deep Atomic to support submissions to the U.S. Department of Energy for a nuclear-powered AI data center campus planned at Idaho National Laboratory, a project that could usher in a new era of energy-efficient computing.On 12th February 2026, Clayco said it would provide early-stage delivery planning, constructability analysis, and integrated design-build expertise as part of the consortium. The firm’s track record in delivering large-scale data centers positions it to help ensure the campus can be designed and built safely, efficiently, and at scale. Bob Clark, Executive Chairman and Founder of Clayco, stressed that confidence in project delivery is essential for a successful DOE submission.In its advisory role, Clayco will align design and construction approaches with the operational requirements of high-density AI workloads, leveraging its integrated delivery model to meet the fast, reliable, and precise needs of modern data centers. The consortium brings together organizations with expertise in advanced nuclear technology, energy systems, and risk modeling, complementing Clayco's practical delivery capabilities. Deep Atomic's approach combines advanced nuclear energy with AI data center infrastructure to ensure reliable, low-carbon power for next-generation computing. The collaboration aims to establish a scalable platform that could position the U.S. as a leader in clean-energy AI infrastructure; Deep Atomic founder and CEO William G. J. Theron said the mission is to accelerate deployment of advanced nuclear power to meet performance and sustainability needs, with Clayco helping integrate innovative energy solutions and execution planning for the DOE submission.

+ FOUNTAIN PARK AS BEACON OF RENEWAL: CLAYCO'S CLARK CALLS FOR COLLABORATIVE RECOVERY

Nearly a year after the May 16 tornado devastated North St. Louis, Clayco CEO Bob Clark says the business community must join with city leaders to forge a unified rebuilding strategy for areas that faced long-standing struggles. In Fountain Park, he voiced cautious optimism about renewal and noted the visible damage from the disaster.Clark said Clayco acted promptly after the storm, coordinating with Mayor Spencer, city services, and local partners. The company’s commitment to long-term rebuilding includes funding and staffing programs with Albion and Washington University to improve quality of life and keep the city moving forward, complemented by ongoing community support.Describing Fountain Park as a potential beacon of hope, Clark highlighted preexisting programs that promote new housing and help families find safe, affordable homes, such as the St. Louis Development Corporation and the Land Clearance for Redevelopment Authority. He emphasized that real recovery starts with the civic community and requires a shared will to move forward, adding that progress happens incrementally—one house at a time, one block at a time.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

+ RYAN COMPANIES [1 News That Matters]

+ MERITAX ADVISORS JOINS RYAN COMPANIES TO ACCELERATE PROPERTY TAX SOLUTIONS

Ryan Companies expanded its property tax services with the acquisition of Meritax Advisors, announced official on the 10th of February 2026. Meritax, a Texas-based commercial property tax consultant, has supported clients across various real estate sectors and manages tens of billions of dollars in assessed value in Texas and beyond.The deal is designed to boost Ryan's capabilities in valuation strategy, litigation management and large-portfolio advisory services, while incorporating Meritax's analytics-driven approach and technology-enabled advocacy to keep pace with evolving property tax systems. Damon Chronis, President of U.S. Operations at Ryan, said the addition brings a disciplined, client-focused team with deep Texas knowledge to the firm.As part of the integration, Meritax partners including Ryan Chismark, Charlie Honea and Mark Kline, along with about 20 other team members, will join Ryan. The move adds to Ryan’s global footprint, which already serves more than 74,000 clients with a workforce of over 7,100 professionals, reinforcing the firm’s leading position in business taxes.

value.Title: CLAYCO CONSTRUCTION COMPANY, INC. - RECENT NEWS OF PEER AND INDUSTRY INSIGHTS

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJM-0963-RSG2-J1WT-00000-00CLAYCO CONSTRUCTION COMPANY, INC. - RECENT NEWS OF PEER AND INDUSTRY INSIGHTS2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:49:10ZLexisNexisSTOCKCLAYCO CONSTRUCTION COMPANY, INC. - RECENT NEWS OF PEER AND INDUSTRY INSIGHTS

DASHBOARD: CLAYCO CONSTRUCTION COMPANY

PermID4296495921Websitehttp://www.claycorp.comIndustryConstructionAddress2199 Innerbelt Business Center Drive Overland, Missouri 63114

ACTIVITIES:

Clayco Construction Company, Inc., founded in 1984 by Bob Clark, is a design-build firm headquartered in St. Louis, Missouri. With over 3,800 employees and $7.6 billion in 2024 revenue, it serves commercial, industrial, and life sciences sectors. Clayco offers architecture, engineering, and construction services through subsidiaries like Lamar Johnson Collaborative and Concrete Strategies. Known for projects like the Novus Headquarters, it emphasizes sustainable, LEED-certified buildings. In 2024, Clayco expanded into renewable energy and data center construction. Its culture fosters innovation, safety, and diversity, with robust training programs. Competing with DPR Construction, Clayco stands out for its integrated approach and fast-track delivery. Recent initiatives include advancing technology-driven construction and eco-friendly practices.

+ CLAYCO CONSTRUCTION COMPANY [3 News That Matters]

+ CHICAGO BUILDER CLAYCO BETS ON SOLAR AND STORAGE WITH NEW NATIONAL ARM

Clayco, a Chicago-based contractor, is expanding into solar and storage with a new national division, announced on 4th March 2026. The venture, known as Clayco Power and Energy, aims to create nearly 1,000 jobs nationwide by 2027 and generate about $300 million in annual revenue.The unit will oversee design, engineering, construction, and procurement for solar and storage projects and is already advancing a 350-acre Manteno, Illinois site, with construction slated to begin this June.The expansion comes as U.S. electricity demand is forecast to rise 15%–20% by 2030, with solar and storage contributing a growing share of new capacity after accounting for roughly 85% of last year’s additions.

+ IDAHO NATIONAL LAB TO HOST NUCLEAR-POWERED AI DATA CENTER: CLAYCO TO LEAD DOE PROPOSAL DESIGN-BUILD EFFORT

Clayco, a leading design-build firm specializing in complex mission-critical facilities, has announced its collaboration with Swiss-American energy company Deep Atomic to support submissions to the U.S. Department of Energy for a nuclear-powered AI data center campus planned at Idaho National Laboratory, a project that could usher in a new era of energy-efficient computing.On 12th February 2026, Clayco said it would provide early-stage delivery planning, constructability analysis, and integrated design-build expertise as part of the consortium. The firm’s track record in delivering large-scale data centers positions it to help ensure the campus can be designed and built safely, efficiently, and at scale. Bob Clark, Executive Chairman and Founder of Clayco, stressed that confidence in project delivery is essential for a successful DOE submission.In its advisory role, Clayco will align design and construction approaches with the operational requirements of high-density AI workloads, leveraging its integrated delivery model to meet the fast, reliable, and precise needs of modern data centers. The consortium brings together organizations with expertise in advanced nuclear technology, energy systems, and risk modeling, complementing Clayco's practical delivery capabilities. Deep Atomic's approach combines advanced nuclear energy with AI data center infrastructure to ensure reliable, low-carbon power for next-generation computing. The collaboration aims to establish a scalable platform that could position the U.S. as a leader in clean-energy AI infrastructure; Deep Atomic founder and CEO William G. J. Theron said the mission is to accelerate deployment of advanced nuclear power to meet performance and sustainability needs, with Clayco helping integrate innovative energy solutions and execution planning for the DOE submission.

+ FOUNTAIN PARK AS BEACON OF RENEWAL: CLAYCO'S CLARK CALLS FOR COLLABORATIVE RECOVERY

Nearly a year after the May 16 tornado devastated North St. Louis, Clayco CEO Bob Clark says the business community must join with city leaders to forge a unified rebuilding strategy for areas that faced long-standing struggles. In Fountain Park, he voiced cautious optimism about renewal and noted the visible damage from the disaster.Clark said Clayco acted promptly after the storm, coordinating with Mayor Spencer, city services, and local partners. The company’s commitment to long-term rebuilding includes funding and staffing programs with Albion and Washington University to improve quality of life and keep the city moving forward, complemented by ongoing community support.Describing Fountain Park as a potential beacon of hope, Clark highlighted preexisting programs that promote new housing and help families find safe, affordable homes, such as the St. Louis Development Corporation and the Land Clearance for Redevelopment Authority. He emphasized that real recovery starts with the civic community and requires a shared will to move forward, adding that progress happens incrementally—one house at a time, one block at a time.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

+ RYAN COMPANIES [2 News That Matters]

+ MERITAX ADVISORS JOINS RYAN COMPANIES TO ACCELERATE PROPERTY TAX SOLUTIONS

Ryan Companies expanded its property tax services with the acquisition of Meritax Advisors, announced official on the 10th of February 2026. Meritax, a Texas-based commercial property tax consultant, has supported clients across various real estate sectors and manages tens of billions of dollars in assessed value in Texas and beyond.The deal is designed to boost Ryan's capabilities in valuation strategy, litigation management and large-portfolio advisory services, while incorporating Meritax's analytics-driven approach and technology-enabled advocacy to keep pace with evolving property tax systems. Damon Chronis, President of U.S. Operations at Ryan, said the addition brings a disciplined, client-focused team with deep Texas knowledge to the firm.As part of the integration, Meritax partners including Ryan Chismark, Charlie Honea and Mark Kline, along with about 20 other team members, will join Ryan. The move adds to Ryan’s global footprint, which already serves more than 74,000 clients with a workforce of over 7,100 professionals, reinforcing the firm’s leading position in business taxes.

+ VETERANS DAY FEATURE: ANNA GRIFFIN CONNECTS MILITARY SERVICE TO CONSTRUCTION EXCELLENCE AT RYAN

Anna Griffin, director of construction for Ryan Companies US, sat down on the AZ Big Podcast to discuss how her military background informs leadership and resilience in the construction industry. The interview, recorded 5 days ago, comes as Veterans Day is observed in Arizona and highlights Griffin's trajectory from Air Force service to overseeing Southwest construction projects. Griffin recently returned from a seven-month deployment to Jordan as aircraft maintenance squadron commander for the 165th Airlift Wing, and she notes that the two fields share common ground: "Construction has so many parallels to my military service," she noted. "You’re still serving — just in a different way. You’re building something lasting, something that supports people and communities." At Ryan Companies, Griffin guides project management teams across healthcare, multifamily housing, and education. After nearly four years at the firm, her leadership helps navigate the complexity of construction environments, with a focus on integrity, accountability, and mission readiness, qualities that served her in the military and now guide civilian projects. The AZ Big Podcast, hosted by Michael Gossie and Amy Lindsey, is sponsored by Burch & Cracchiolo and aims to spotlight how strong leadership and collaboration drive success across industries in Arizona's business landscape. Griffin's episode is timely as the state honors its veterans and recognizes the valuable skills they contribute to the workforce. Listeners are encouraged to tune in to hear her compelling story and understand how veterans like her are making meaningful contributions to sectors such as construction. As she continues her work with Ryan Companies, her journey demonstrates the strength and adaptability of veterans transitioning into fresh roles, underscoring that the capabilities developed in military service are transferable across fields.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

value.Title: CLAYCO CONSTRUCTION COMPANY, INC. - RECENT PEER AND INDUSTRY INSIGHTS

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJ0-7JB3-SCSG-X0BT-00000-00CLAYCO CONSTRUCTION COMPANY, INC. - RECENT PEER AND INDUSTRY INSIGHTS2026-05-08T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:49:10ZLexisNexisSTOCKCLAYCO CONSTRUCTION COMPANY, INC. - RECENT PEER AND INDUSTRY INSIGHTS

DASHBOARD: CLAYCO CONSTRUCTION COMPANY

PermID4296495921Websitehttp://www.claycorp.comIndustryConstructionAddress2199 Innerbelt Business Center Drive Overland, Missouri 63114

ACTIVITIES:

Clayco Construction Company, Inc., founded in 1984 by Bob Clark, is a design-build firm headquartered in St. Louis, Missouri. With over 3,800 employees and $7.6 billion in 2024 revenue, it serves commercial, industrial, and life sciences sectors. Clayco offers architecture, engineering, and construction services through subsidiaries like Lamar Johnson Collaborative and Concrete Strategies. Known for projects like the Novus Headquarters, it emphasizes sustainable, LEED-certified buildings. In 2024, Clayco expanded into renewable energy and data center construction. Its culture fosters innovation, safety, and diversity, with robust training programs. Competing with DPR Construction, Clayco stands out for its integrated approach and fast-track delivery. Recent initiatives include advancing technology-driven construction and eco-friendly practices.

+ CLAYCO CONSTRUCTION COMPANY [3 News That Matters]

+ CHICAGO BUILDER CLAYCO BETS ON SOLAR AND STORAGE WITH NEW NATIONAL ARM

Clayco, a Chicago-based contractor, is expanding into solar and storage with a new national division, announced on 4th March 2026. The venture, known as Clayco Power and Energy, aims to create nearly 1,000 jobs nationwide by 2027 and generate about $300 million in annual revenue.The unit will oversee design, engineering, construction, and procurement for solar and storage projects and is already advancing a 350-acre Manteno, Illinois site, with construction slated to begin this June.The expansion comes as U.S. electricity demand is forecast to rise 15%–20% by 2030, with solar and storage contributing a growing share of new capacity after accounting for roughly 85% of last year’s additions.

+ IDAHO NATIONAL LAB TO HOST NUCLEAR-POWERED AI DATA CENTER: CLAYCO TO LEAD DOE PROPOSAL DESIGN-BUILD EFFORT

Clayco, a leading design-build firm specializing in complex mission-critical facilities, has announced its collaboration with Swiss-American energy company Deep Atomic to support submissions to the U.S. Department of Energy for a nuclear-powered AI data center campus planned at Idaho National Laboratory, a project that could usher in a new era of energy-efficient computing.On 12th February 2026, Clayco said it would provide early-stage delivery planning, constructability analysis, and integrated design-build expertise as part of the consortium. The firm’s track record in delivering large-scale data centers positions it to help ensure the campus can be designed and built safely, efficiently, and at scale. Bob Clark, Executive Chairman and Founder of Clayco, stressed that confidence in project delivery is essential for a successful DOE submission.In its advisory role, Clayco will align design and construction approaches with the operational requirements of high-density AI workloads, leveraging its integrated delivery model to meet the fast, reliable, and precise needs of modern data centers. The consortium brings together organizations with expertise in advanced nuclear technology, energy systems, and risk modeling, complementing Clayco's practical delivery capabilities. Deep Atomic's approach combines advanced nuclear energy with AI data center infrastructure to ensure reliable, low-carbon power for next-generation computing. The collaboration aims to establish a scalable platform that could position the U.S. as a leader in clean-energy AI infrastructure; Deep Atomic founder and CEO William G. J. Theron said the mission is to accelerate deployment of advanced nuclear power to meet performance and sustainability needs, with Clayco helping integrate innovative energy solutions and execution planning for the DOE submission.

+ FOUNTAIN PARK AS BEACON OF RENEWAL: CLAYCO'S CLARK CALLS FOR COLLABORATIVE RECOVERY

Nearly a year after the May 16 tornado devastated North St. Louis, Clayco CEO Bob Clark says the business community must join with city leaders to forge a unified rebuilding strategy for areas that faced long-standing struggles. In Fountain Park, he voiced cautious optimism about renewal and noted the visible damage from the disaster.Clark said Clayco acted promptly after the storm, coordinating with Mayor Spencer, city services, and local partners. The company’s commitment to long-term rebuilding includes funding and staffing programs with Albion and Washington University to improve quality of life and keep the city moving forward, complemented by ongoing community support.Describing Fountain Park as a potential beacon of hope, Clark highlighted preexisting programs that promote new housing and help families find safe, affordable homes, such as the St. Louis Development Corporation and the Land Clearance for Redevelopment Authority. He emphasized that real recovery starts with the civic community and requires a shared will to move forward, adding that progress happens incrementally—one house at a time, one block at a time.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

+ RYAN COMPANIES [2 News That Matters]

+ MERITAX ADVISORS JOINS RYAN COMPANIES TO ACCELERATE PROPERTY TAX SOLUTIONS

Ryan Companies expanded its property tax services with the acquisition of Meritax Advisors, announced official on the 10th of February 2026. Meritax, a Texas-based commercial property tax consultant, has supported clients across various real estate sectors and manages tens of billions of dollars in assessed value in Texas and beyond.The deal is designed to boost Ryan's capabilities in valuation strategy, litigation management and large-portfolio advisory services, while incorporating Meritax's analytics-driven approach and technology-enabled advocacy to keep pace with evolving property tax systems. Damon Chronis, President of U.S. Operations at Ryan, said the addition brings a disciplined, client-focused team with deep Texas knowledge to the firm.As part of the integration, Meritax partners including Ryan Chismark, Charlie Honea and Mark Kline, along with about 20 other team members, will join Ryan. The move adds to Ryan’s global footprint, which already serves more than 74,000 clients with a workforce of over 7,100 professionals, reinforcing the firm’s leading position in business taxes.

+ VETERANS DAY FEATURE: ANNA GRIFFIN CONNECTS MILITARY SERVICE TO CONSTRUCTION EXCELLENCE AT RYAN

Anna Griffin, director of construction for Ryan Companies US, sat down on the AZ Big Podcast to discuss how her military background informs leadership and resilience in the construction industry. The interview, recorded 5 days ago, comes as Veterans Day is observed in Arizona and highlights Griffin's trajectory from Air Force service to overseeing Southwest construction projects. Griffin recently returned from a seven-month deployment to Jordan as aircraft maintenance squadron commander for the 165th Airlift Wing, and she notes that the two fields share common ground: "Construction has so many parallels to my military service," she noted. "You’re still serving — just in a different way. You’re building something lasting, something that supports people and communities." At Ryan Companies, Griffin guides project management teams across healthcare, multifamily housing, and education. After nearly four years at the firm, her leadership helps navigate the complexity of construction environments, with a focus on integrity, accountability, and mission readiness, qualities that served her in the military and now guide civilian projects. The AZ Big Podcast, hosted by Michael Gossie and Amy Lindsey, is sponsored by Burch & Cracchiolo and aims to spotlight how strong leadership and collaboration drive success across industries in Arizona's business landscape. Griffin's episode is timely as the state honors its veterans and recognizes the valuable skills they contribute to the workforce. Listeners are encouraged to tune in to hear her compelling story and understand how veterans like her are making meaningful contributions to sectors such as construction. As she continues her work with Ryan Companies, her journey demonstrates the strength and adaptability of veterans transitioning into fresh roles, underscoring that the capabilities developed in military service are transferable across fields.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++
